content.text: Text and headings are the backbone of every web page. They establish the document's information hierarchy, make content accessible to screen readers and search engines, and give browsers the semantic cues needed to present content correctly. Understanding which element to reach for — and why — is a foundational skill for writing clean, maintainable HTML.
. They are block-level elements that introduce sections of content. The number reflects importance:
is the top-level heading — the title of the page or document — and each subsequent level introduces a subsection of the one above it.

attrs.title: HTML Text & Heading Elements

content.text: ) are block-level elements that label sections of content. They carry two roles at once: visual presentation (browsers apply progressively smaller bold text by default) and semantic structure (they tell assistive technologies and search engine crawlers how the page is organised). Paragraph elements (
) are the primary container for prose. Inline elements such as
, and their cousins run inside block containers and annotate individual words or phrases.
Every page should have exactly one
that names the page's main topic. Subsections become
, and so on. Do not skip levels for cosmetic reasons — if you want an
to appear smaller, adjust CSS instead of inserting an out-of-order heading.

Screen readers expose a heading outline to keyboard users, letting them jump directly to any section. A broken outline — levels skipped, headings used just to make text big — forces those users to read the page linearly, which is a significant accessibility regression.

Browsers apply a default stylesheet to all HTML. The heading sizes below come from that stylesheet. All of these defaults should be overridden in a real project's CSS — what matters is the semantic order, not the default size.
is a block-level container for a unit of prose. Browsers add default top and bottom margin so adjacent paragraphs are visually separated. You cannot nest block-level elements inside a
inside an open paragraph, the parser implicitly closes the outer one first. Empty
elements used for spacing are an anti-pattern; use margin or padding instead.
HTML5 separates inline elements into two camps: semantic (convey meaning to assistive technology) and presentational (visual only). Using the right one matters for screen readers.
Strong importance — screen readers may stress this
Stress emphasis — changes the meaning of the sentence
Bold without semantic weight (e.g. product names, keywords)
Italic without emphasis (e.g. technical terms, foreign phrases)
A useful rule of thumb: if the meaning of the text would change when read aloud without visual emphasis, use
. If you are styling for aesthetics alone, use

— the title of a creative work (book, film, song). Note:

— a machine-readable date or time. The

Angular is a platform for building mobile and desktop web applications.

As the Angular team says, Angular is a platform, not just a library.
Angular templates follow HTML grammar, so all the elements above work exactly the same way inside a component's template. Dynamic values are interpolated with double-curly braces:

{{ pageTitle }}

Welcome, {{ user.name }}. You have {{ notifications }} new messages.

{{ term }} {{ article.publishedAt | date:'longDate' }}
Angular's template engine HTML-escapes all interpolated values by default. If you bind
tag, it will be rendered as the literal string, not executed. This makes interpolation safe for untrusted content.
Occasionally you need to render rich-text HTML stored as a string — for example, CMS content or a markdown-to-HTML converter output. Angular provides the
binding for this, but it sanitises the value through
before inserting it into the DOM:
only when you control and validate the HTML source yourself. Passing arbitrary user input through this method opens an XSS vector. When in doubt, prefer interpolation (
Angular's control flow syntax makes it straightforward to render text conditionally based on component state:

@if (article.isBreaking) { BREAKING: } {{ article.headline }}

{{ results.length }} @if (results.length === 1) { result } @else { results } found for {{ query }}

@for (word of article.body.split(' '); track $index) { @if (query && word.toLowerCase().startsWith(query.toLowerCase())) { {{ word }} } @else { {{ word }} } {{ ' ' }} }

Quick reference: choosing the right element

HTML collapses all consecutive whitespace (spaces, tabs, newlines) in normal flow to a single space. This is called whitespace normalisation and it means you can format your source HTML with any indentation style without affecting the rendered output. The following two markup fragments produce identical output:

The quick brown fox

The quick brown fox
elements, where all whitespace is preserved exactly. The CSS property
lets you control this behaviour per-element:
preserves whitespace but still wraps long lines.

forces a line break within flow content. It is a void element (no closing tag) and should only be used where the line break is semantically meaningful — inside a poem stanza, a postal address, or a verse. It is not a substitute for paragraph spacing or section separation:

Acme Corporation
123 Innovation Drive
San Francisco, CA 94107

Resetting browser text defaults with CSS

Every browser ships a built-in stylesheet (the user-agent stylesheet) that applies default margins, padding, and font sizes to headings and text elements. In a real Angular project, you will almost always reset or normalise these defaults before applying your design system. There are two common approaches:

— removes all browser defaults, giving you a completely blank canvas. The trade-off is that you must re-declare every desired style. Popular resets include Eric Meyer's reset and the "modern" reset by Andy Bell.
— corrects inconsistencies between browsers but preserves sensible defaults. Normalize.css is the most widely used. Angular's default project template includes it via the
when you choose a CSS framework at project creation.
A minimal heading reset that is common in design systems:

remains the most important heading in the accessibility tree. It simply separates the semantic level from its default visual style, which is the right approach: define sizes in your design system's typography scale rather than relying on browser defaults.
